truth.papers.A48.title: Chemical Composition Optimization for Austenitic Steels of the Fe-Mn-Al-C System
truth.papers.A48.paper_scope: Define composition limits to obtain an optimum microstructural state without embrittling phases, and determine composition ranges for structural applications with UTS ≥ 1100 MPa and impact toughness > 50 J/cm².

prediction.papers.A48.title: Chemical Composition Optimization for Austenitic Steels of the Fe-Mn-Al-C System
prediction.papers.A48.paper_scope: Determination of composition limits for Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels to obtain an optimum microstructural state free of embrittling phases and acceptable mechanical properties for structural applications, specifically UTS greater than 1100 MPa and U-notch impact toughness K_CU greater than 50 J/cm².

truth.alloys.alloy_fe29mn9al09c.alloys_notes: Typical nominal composition used as baseline.

prediction.alloys.alloy_fe29mn9al09c.alloys_notes: Systematic study of 31 experimental melts covering Al 3-10%, C 0.3-1.2%, and Mn 24-34%. The optimized composition range for structural applications, accounting for industrial liquation in heavy ingots, is Al 9-9.5%, C 0.9-0.95%, and Mn 28.5-30.5%. Broader fundamental limits without liquation considerations are Al 6.2-9.7%, C 0.7-1%, and Mn 25-31%.

truth.processes.proc_quench1150_age550.descrip: Water quench from 1150°C followed by aging at 550°C for 16 h.
truth.processes.proc_quench1050_age550.descrip: Heated to 1050°C, water quenched to room temperature, then aged at 550°C for 16 h.
truth.processes.proc_quench1050_age550.process: Used for samples in Fig. 6 (solid curves) and Fig. 7.
truth.processes.proc_quench1050.description: Heated to 1050°C and water quenched to room temperature (no aging).

prediction.processes.proc_quench1050_age550.descrip: Induction melting in a 100 kg furnace, electroslag remelting, homogenization at 1150°C for 6-8 h, shaping to circular and square rods, solution treatment at 1050°C with water quenching, and aging at 550°C for 16 h.
prediction.processes.proc_quench1050_age550.process: Cast ingot-electrodes were repeatedly melted and refined in an electric-slag remelting furnace. Hot working and shaping to rods (12 mm diameter circular; 14 × 14 mm square) were performed after homogenization. For cast steels with up to 12% delta-ferrite, hot working within 1150-1170°C accompanies delta-ferrite dissolution. Jet spraying of liquid metal in argon atmosphere was also mentioned as a method to suppress liquation and avoid delta-ferrite up to 12% Al.

prediction.processing_steps.sample_c09_q1050a.proce: Standard aging condition to precipitate kappa-phase particles for strengthening. Aging within 400-850°C was also investigated for magnetic response and phase transformation kinetics.

prediction.structures.sample_c09_q1050a.overall_str: Fully austenitic matrix in the optimized condition; microstructural optimization requires absence of delta-ferrite, grain-boundary carbide networks, and beta-Mn precipitates. Kappa-phase precipitation provides age hardening.
prediction.structures.sample_c09_q1050a.number_of_p: 1 to 3 depending on composition and thermal history
prediction.structures.sample_c09_q1050a.microstruct: increases with Al content beyond approximately 7%
homogeneous precipitation within grains for Al greater than approximately 6.2-7%
up to approximately 12% in cast high-Al alloys
grain boundary network in low-Al or high-C aged conditions

truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.technique: metallography, X-ray diffraction, magnetic saturation measurement
truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.purpose: determine delta-ferrite content, phase identification, measure saturation magnetization 4πJs
truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.key_findi: d-ferrite appears when Al > 10% (conventional melting) or > 12% (rapid solidification); k-phase precipitation occurs when Al > 6.2% at 550-650 °C; saturation magnetization increases with C content and aging temperature/time.

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_c09_q105: qualitative assessment of aging process intensity

Saturation magnetization (4πJ_s) measured after aging for 16 and 100 h at temperatures between 400°C and 850°C. Carbon was identified as the leading element at the onset of aging; the extent of aging depends on aluminum content.

truth.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: predict UTS and impact toughness K_CU as functions of composition (Al, C, Mn)
Used to determine composition limits for UTS ≥ 1100 MPa and K_CU ≥ 50 J/cm². Resulting optimized ranges: Al 9-9.5%, C 0.9-0.95%, Mn 28.5-30.5%.

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Experimental data from 31 compositions were used to construct and validate the response surfaces.
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: correlating UTS and K_CU with independent composition variables Mn, Al, and C

Spatial figures limited by surfaces of equal-content levels were plotted. The model established that UTS of at least 1100 MPa together with K_CU of at least 50 J/cm² are attained for C 0.84-0.99%, Al 8.8-9.7%, and Mn 28.5-30.5%. For heavy ingots, narrower practical limits are Al 9-9.5% and C 0.9-0.95%.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Fig. 2 shows k-phase (carbide) precipitation along grain boundaries in a steel with 4.6 wt.% Al after aging at 600°C for 16 h, which severely reduces impact toughness.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Fig. 3 reveals d-ferrite morphology in a steel with 10.5 wt.% Al: (a) as-cast state shows d-ferrite; (b) after hot deformation at 1150-1170°C d-ferrite is partially dissolved.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Rapid solidification (jet spraying in Ar) produces small spherical particles (<500 µm) and suppresses d-ferrite up to 12% Al (with 0.9% C and 29% Mn). At 14% Al, d-ferrite volume fraction is about 10% (Fig. 4).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Fig. 5 reports saturation magnetization 4πJs (in Gauss, G) after aging at various temperatures and times for Fe-9Al-30Mn steels with different C contents. Example values (approximate from graph): 0.8%C aged 450°C/100h ~1500 G, 550°C/16h ~300 G; 1.06%C aged 450°C/100h ~2400 G, 550°C/16h ~1000 G.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: The paper defines composition limits for optimum microstructural state: 6.2% < Al < 9.7%, 0.7% < C < 1.0%, 25% < Mn < 31%.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Further narrowed limits for structural applications: Al 9-9.5%, C 0.9-0.95%, Mn 28.5-30.5%.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Fig. 1 data (Al effect, water quench from 1150°C and aging at 550°C for 16 h): For 0.85% C, UTS increases from 800 MPa (3% Al) to 1050 MPa (10% Al); for 0.95% C, UTS increases from 800 MPa (3% Al) to 1250 MPa (10% Al). Ductility remains approximately 45% across the entire Al range for both C levels. U-notch impact toughness (K_CU) peaks at 180 J/cm² for 6-7% Al (0.85% C) and 160 J/cm² for 6-7% Al (0.95% C), dropping sharply to 10 J/cm² at 3% Al (0.85% C) and 7 J/cm² at 3% Al (0.95% C), and to 10 J/cm² at 10% Al (0.85% C) and 60 J/cm² at 10% Al (0.95% C).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Fig. 5 data (C effect on aging process, Al 9%, Mn 30%): Saturation magnetization (4πJ_s) increases with carbon content and decreases with higher aging temperatures. Representative values after 16 h aging: at 550°C, 0.8% C gives approximately 300 G, 0.92% C gives approximately 600 G, 0.98% C gives approximately 900 G, and 1.06% C gives approximately 1000 G. At 500°C/16h, values range from approximately 150 G (0.8% C) to approximately 750 G (1.06% C). For the same Al content, 4πJ_s increases only up to a certain carbon concentration; raising this limit requires higher Al.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Fig. 6 data (C effect on mechanical properties, Al 9%, Mn 30%): The figure presents properties for water-quenched (dashed curves) and aged 550°C/16 h (solid curves) conditions. In the aged state, UTS reaches 1250 MPa at 0.9% C and 1350 MPa at 1.1% C; 0.2% YS reaches 1050 MPa at 0.9% C and 1200 MPa at 1.1% C; ductility is 45% at 0.9% C but drops to 10% at 1.1% C; U-notch impact toughness (K_CU) is 110 J/cm² at 0.9% C and 30 J/cm² at 1.1% C. Carbon concentrations exceeding 1% in the aged state cause grain-boundary carbide precipitation and reduce K_CU by at least 40 J/cm². For C lower than 0.7%, delta-ferrite appears.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Fig. 7 data (Mn effect, Al 9%, C 0.9%, WQ from 1050°C and aged 550°C/16 h): UTS peaks at 1200 MPa for 26% Mn and decreases to 950 MPa for 34% Mn; 0.2% YS is 1000 MPa at 26-28% Mn and 880 MPa at 34% Mn; elongation is highest at 60% for 30% Mn; K_CU is 100-115 J/cm² for Mn 24-32% but drops sharply to 35 J/cm² at 34% Mn due to beta-Mn precipitation after aging.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Microstructural constraints and phase stability: Homogeneous nucleation of kappa-phase during aging at 550°C occurs for Al greater than approximately 7% (the critical Al concentration decreases with higher C). For Al less than approximately 6.2%, carbides precipitate as a continuous network along grain boundaries during aging (Fig. 2), which is highly detrimental to impact toughness. Delta-ferrite appears for Al greater than 10% in cast structures but dissolves during hot working at 1150-1170°C for contents up to approximately 12%. Beta-Mn precipitates after long-time aging at 550°C when Mn exceeds 31-32%, causing extreme brittleness.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Solidification and liquation control: In open-air melting, delta-ferrite increases with ingot weight for Al greater than 9.5%. Electroslag remelting reduces liquation such that delta-ferrite is absent in hot-worked semifinished products for Al less than 9.5% (cross sections up to 105 × 105 mm). Jet spraying of liquid metal in argon atmosphere suppresses liquation and avoids delta-ferrite even at 12% Al (0.9% C, 29% Mn); at 14% Al, delta-ferrite may reach approximately 10% volume fraction after working.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Carbon solid-solution hardening relationship: In the as-quenched condition, each 0.1% increase in carbon content increases the yield stress by 30 to 40 MPa.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Figure 2 caption and image description indicate kappa-phase precipitation in steel with 4.6 wt.% Al after aging at 600°C for 16 h, showing polygonal crystalline structures. Figure 3 shows delta-ferrite in 10.5 wt.% Al steel in as-cast and deformed conditions. Figure 4 shows granulated and pressed steel quenched from 1100°C with 9.8% Al and 14% Al. Exact precipitate dimensions, ferrite morphology details, and grain size distributions are not extractable from the provided text and captions alone.
